coredata.dc:description: Caffeic acid (CA), a natural phenolic acid compound, exhibits various beneficial effects, including antioxidant, antibacterial, and potential anticancer activities. However, excessive intake of CA may pose certain risks, and undegraded CA residues in wastewater can lead to environmental pollution. In this study, a novel fluorescence-colorimetric dual-mode probe with double emission centers, BA-CDs@Eu-MOF, is constructed for the rapid detection of caffeic acid by incorporating borate-functionalized carbon dots (BA-CDs) into a europium-based organic framework (Eu-MOF). The boric acid groups in the BA-CDs specifically interact with the o-dihydroxyl groups of CA molecules, resulting in high sensitivity and selectivity for CA recognition. Upon the addition of CA, the blue fluorescence of the probe gradually increases at 423 nm, while the red fluorescence decreases at 615 nm accordingly. A linear relationship is observed between CA concentration and the fluorescence intensity ratio (I423/I615) in the range of 0–60 µM with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.079 µM. Furthermore, the probe has successfully been applied to detect CA in real samples, including coffee bean, green tea, tap water and river water, with satisfactory recovery rates ranging from 97.6 % to 104.8 %. Additionally, by cooperation of the BA-CDs@Eu-MOF probe with smartphone RGB software a visual colorimetric sensing platform for detection of CA is constructed, which offers a novel strategy for instant and on-site monitoring of caffeic acid. These dual mode sensings can be calibrated and validated with each other, thus ensuring high accuracy.

head.ce:title: Encapsulating boron-doped carbon dots into Eu-MOF as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for the rapid detection of caffeic acid

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Caffeic acid (CA), a natural phenolic acid compound, exhibits various beneficial effects, including antioxidant, antibacterial, and potential anticancer activities. However, excessive intake of CA may pose certain risks, and undegraded CA residues in wastewater can lead to environmental pollution. In this study, a novel fluorescence-colorimetric dual-mode probe with double emission centers, BA-CDs@Eu-MOF, is constructed for the rapid detection of caffeic acid by incorporating borate-functionalized carbon dots (BA-CDs) into a europium-based organic framework (Eu-MOF). The boric acid groups in the BA-CDs specifically interact with the o-dihydroxyl groups of CA molecules, resulting in high sensitivity and selectivity for CA recognition. Upon the addition of CA, the blue fluorescence of the probe gradually increases at 423 nm, while the red fluorescence decreases at 615 nm accordingly. A linear relationship is observed between CA concentration and the fluorescence intensity ratio (I423/I615) in the range of 0–60 µM with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.079 µM. Furthermore, the probe has successfully been applied to detect CA in real samples, including coffee bean, green tea, tap water and river water, with satisfactory recovery rates ranging from 97.6 % to 104.8 %. Additionally, by cooperation of the BA-CDs@Eu-MOF probe with smartphone RGB software a visual colorimetric sensing platform for detection of CA is constructed, which offers a novel strategy for instant and on-site monitoring of caffeic acid. These dual mode sensings can be calibrated and validated with each other, thus ensuring high accuracy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Caffeic acid (CA), a natural polyphenolic compound, exhibits multiple biological activities and has a wide range of applications. It is found in coffee, tea, fruits and certain medicinal plants, playing a significant role in various fields such as food, medicine and cosmetics. CA is beneficial to health due to its various medical functions, including antioxidant [1,2], hypoglycemic [3], hemostatic [4], anti-inflammatory [5], antibacterial [6], and neuroprotective [7] effects. Additionally, it also serves as a natural preservative [8] and is one of the core pharmacodynamic components [9,10] found in traditional Chinese medicines, such as dandelion and honeysuckle. Although moderate use of caffeic acid is beneficial for human health, excessive intake can cause serious irritation reactions and intestinal diseases [11]. More importantly, residual caffeic acid does not undergo natural oxidation in air or water, posing serious health risks including cancer and other fatal consequences [12,13]. As one of the most challenging phenolic compounds to decompose, its presence in wastewater can lead to water pollution and adversely affect plants and soil in the environment [14]. In addition, in foods such as coffee, wine, and fruits, CA significantly affects flavor and quality [15]. Therefore, the use of CA must be carefully controlled within a safe range [16,17]. The appropriate dosage listed in the National Food Consumption Survey (NFCS) is 0.3–0.5 g per day. It is reported that coffeic acid contents in green tea and coffee are ∼261 and ∼900 mg/kg [18]. Consequently, establishing a sensitive sensing platform for the rapid detection of CA holds significant practical importance in food science and environmental applications.

Traditional detection techniques such as thin-layer chromatography (TLC) [19], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [20] and high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [21] are known for their high sensitivity and excellent selectivity. However, these methods necessitate professional operators, expensive experimental instruments, complicated pretreatment steps and time-consuming operation [22]. The utilization of fluorescence sensing strategies presents several advantages, including enhanced sensitivity, accuracy, rapid response times, and user-friendliness, making them particularly suitable for the online monitoring of pollutants [23,24]. For example, Ali et al. utilized a copper nanocluster protected by dithioerythritol (DTT@CuNCs) as a novel fluorescence probe to determine rapidly CA [13]. Their method demonstrated high accuracy and precision due to La3 + induced aggregation effect.

As we know, probe material is a key factor determining the performance of fluorescence sensors. Due to high specific surface areas [25], tunable compositions and versatile structures [26], metal organic frameworks (MOFs), a class of porous materials characterized by periodic network structures formed by coordination self-assembling of metal ions or metal clusters and organic ligands [27], have showed huge application potentials in gas sensing [28,29], molecular sieving [30], catalysis [31], electrical devices [32], adsorption [33–36]. Especially, as fluorescent sensing substrates [37–39], MOFs materials have shown extraordinary appeal. For example, R. Kataria's team ever innovatively exploited nanozyme property of Co-MOF to detect sensitively Fe3+ in environmental water samples [40] and developed fingerprint characteristics of Zn-MOF for detection of explosives [41]. So far, many organic compounds, including biomolecules [42] and explosives [43,44], have been measured using MOF based fluorescence sensing platforms.

Carbon dots (CDs), a class of carbon nanomaterials, also have garnered significant attention in recent years due to their distinctive fluorescence properties [45]. Typically, CDs are defined as carbon particles with a size of less than 10 nm with excellent dispersibility. They may always contain various functional groups, including carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino groups or aromatic rings [46]. In addition to possessing a high quantum yield and adjustable emission wavelengths, CDs exhibit remarkable photostability, biocompatibility, chemical inertness, negligible cytotoxicity, and ease of surface modification among other characteristics [47]. Based on previous studies, CDs can be utilized independently for fluorescence detection [48] or be incorporated into composite materials [49,50] or molecular imprints [51] for fluorescence detection. The boric acid carbon dots (BA-CDs) discussed in this paper represent a novel class of carbon-based nanomaterials that integrate the functional properties of CDs and boric acid groups [52]. These materials possess distinctive optical, chemical, and biological properties, making them widely applicable in various fields, including sensing, bioimaging and catalysis [53]. The introduction of boric acid groups endows them with specific recognition and binding capabilities, particularly for cis-dihydroxy compounds. Given that BA-CDs are subject to aggregation-induced quenching [54,55], we opted to encapsulate BA-CDs within MOFs. Our aim was to harness the advantages of lanthanide elements, including their diverse energy level structures, extended fluorescence lifetimes, and distinct emission peaks [56], to develop a ratiometric fluorescence sensor.

Based on above consideration, this study developed a ratiometric fluorescence sensor, BA-CDs@Eu-MOF, for the detection of CA. The specific synthesis steps are illustrated in Scheme 1 . In this composite material, boric acid group-containing BA-CDs were incorporated into the Eu-MOF framework. The specific interaction between the boric acid groups in BA-CDs and the cis-ortho-dihydroxy groups of CA resulted in an enhancement in the probe's fluorescence intensity at 423 nm, and simultaneous weakening of the red emission at 615 nm. Consequently, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor was proposed for the detection of CA. In addition, when CA was introduced, the color of the system rapidly changed from red to blue under ultraviolet light, which facilitated the visual detection of CA. The results indicated that the constructed BA-CDs@Eu-MOF fluorescence sensor was capable of detecting of CA in real samples, such as coffee bean, green tea, tap water and river water, with high sensitivity and a low detection limit.
The reagents and apparatus used in the experiment are given in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the boronic acid functionalized carbon dots (BA-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: BA-CDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal carbonization method reported previously [57]. In brief, 0.5 g of phenylboronic acid was dissolved in 50 mL of ultrapure water. Subsequently, 0.1 M sodium hydroxide was added under stirring to adjust the pH to 9.0, accompanied by nitrogen bubbling for 15 min. Finally, the mixture was placed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 160 °C for 8 h. After cooled down to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged at a speed of 10,000 rpm for 30 min. The resulting BA-CDs solution was purified by dialysis using a dialysis membrane (Molecular weight cutoff 1000) for 24 h. The purified BA-CDs were stored at 4 °C.

The composite material was prepared by referring to the previously disclosed methods [58,59]. Typically, 44.6 mg (0.1 mmol) Eu(NO3)3·6H2O and 21 mg (0.1 mmol) H3BTC were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water and 10 mL ethanol, respectively. After the two solutions are thoroughly mixed, 5 mL of BA-CDs (4 mmol) were added. The resultant mixture solution was stirred at room temperature for 1 h. Finally, the product was separated by centrifugation, washed with ethanol and water, respectively, and then dried overnight in a vacuum oven at 60 °C to obtain a white precipitate. The resulting product was called BA-CDs@Eu-MOF. Eu-MOF was synthesized using the same procedure, except that BN-CDs were not added.

To investigate the response of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF to CA, a series of aqueous solutions of CA with varying concentrations (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50 and 60 μM) were prepared and an equal quantity of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF was then added. After a certain reaction time, fluorescence spectrophotometry was employed for detection under excitation at 305 nm. To evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference ability of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF towards CA, an equal quantity of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF was respectively added into the aqueous solutions containing different substances such as Zn2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, K+, Na+, Cu2+, Ca2+, leucine, threonine, glutamate, phenylalanine, aspartate, L-tyrosine, L-valine, pyrogallic acid, lauric acid, tartaric acid, citric acid, stearic acid, glucose and CA. Fluorescence spectra were recorded under excitation at 305 nm. Then, 60 μM of CA was added into above mixture solutions, respectively, until the concentration of each interfering substance was 10 times that of CA. The emission spectra were recorded under excitation at 305 nm for anti-interference testing. All the above tests were carried out in triplicate (n = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CA in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Coffee, green tea, tap water and river water were selected as actual samples to evaluate the feasibility of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF as a ratiometric probe for the detection of CA. Coffee beans and green tea leaves were purchased from local supermarket. 0.1 g of coffee bean or green tea sample was ground into powder, ultrasonically treated in 50 mL of deionized water for 30 min, and then stood overnight. After filtration, 1 mL of the filtrate was diluted 50 times for detection. The determination of CA was carried out by the standard addition method. Recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) were calculated from the experimental results (RSD is the value obtained by dividing the standard deviation by the corresponding mean and multiplying by 100 %). We also collected tap water and Yellow river water for testing. All water samples were treated with a 0.22 μm filter membrane before use. Different volumes of known concentrations of CA were added to the processed water samples, and then an equivalent amount BA-CDs@Eu-MOF was added, respectively for determination. All samples were measured in triplicate (n = 3) at an excitation wavelength of 305 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the portable smartphone sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, a series of CA solutions with a concentration gradient were prepared and added to centrifuge tubes. Subsequently, an aqueous solution of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF was added into each tube. To minimize errors arising from environmental background interference, all centrifuge tubes were placed in a darkroom. Under ultraviolet light irradiation, photographs of the BA-CDs@Eu-MOF probe solutions containing varying concentrations of CA were taken using a smartphone. Subsequently, the observed colors were converted into RGB tricolor values (R+G+B) utilizing the color recognition software of the smartphone. Finally, the linear relationship between the R/B value and the CA concentration was developed. Furthermore, the CA concentration can be determined based on the established linear relationship.

The morphologies and microstructures of Eu-MOF and BA-CDs@Eu-MOF were observed and analyzed by using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Fig. 1(a–c), Eu-MOF exhibited a straw-like morphology composed of rod-like structures. It could be seen from Fig. 1(d–f) that BA-CDs@Eu-MOF had a dispersive rod-like structure, which proved that the introduction of BA-CDs did not destroy the original structure of Eu-MOF and simultaneously eliminated the accumulation of Eu-MOF. Fig. 1(g) presented the TEM image of BA-CDs. It was observed that the particle size of BA-CDs was approximately 3–4 nm. By observing the TEM image of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF in Fig. 1(h), it was found that the BA-CDs have been successfully embedded in Eu-MOF. The elemental mapping of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF were analyzed by X-ray energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). As shown in Fig. 1 (i–m), BA-CDs@Eu-MOF was mainly composed of C, O, Eu and B elements, which was consistent with the expected.

XRD technique was utilized to analyze crystallinity and phase composition of both Eu-MOF and BA-CDs@Eu-MOF. As seen in Fig. 2(a), the prepared Eu-MOF showed well-defined and sharp diffraction peaks, which exactly matched those of the simulated Eu-MOF, without any additional peak being detected. Moreover, the peak positions of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF were in close agreement with those of Eu-MOF. This implied that the composite material BA-CDs@Eu-MOF possessed high purity and good crystallinity, and the encapsulation of BA-CDs had not affected the crystal structure of Eu-MOF. Subsequently, XPS spectra of Eu-MOF and BA-CDs@Eu-MOF were tested and compared. Their XPS survey spectra showed in Fig. 2(b) clearly showed existence of C, O, and Eu elements in both Eu-MOF and BA-CDs@Eu-MOF. Moreover, the B element from BA-CDs was also detected in BA-CDs@Eu-MOF. The high-resolution spectra of Eu 3d for Eu-MOF and BA-CDs@Eu-MOF (Fig. 2(c)) clearly exhibited Eu 3d3/2 and Eu 3d5/2 spin-orbital doublets at 1165.1 eV and 1135.8 eV, respectively. Among them, the Eu 3d3/2 peak was decomposed into two components attributed to Eu3+ and Eu2+, respectively. The aforesaid situation verified that BA-CDs have been successfully encapsulated into Eu-MOF without any negative impact on the structure of Eu-MOF, implying the successful preparation of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF composite material.

To further analyze the surface groups of the composite material, FT-IR spectra of H3BTC, Eu-MOF and BA-CDs@Eu-MOF were measured and the results were depicted in Fig. 2(d). Compared with Eu-MOF, the broad hydroxyl peak (ranging from 2540 to 3590 cm⁻¹) corresponding to -COOH in H3BTC disappeared, which indicated the complete deprotonation of H3BTC. The asymmetric stretching vibration of -COO- in BTC at 1721 cm−1 blue-shifted by 106 cm−1 when it coordinated with Eu3+. It is worth noting that compared to Eu-MOF, BA-CDs@Eu-MOF exhibited the characteristic peak of B-O-H at 1048 cm−1, demonstrating the successful encapsulation of BA-CDs into Eu-MOF. The hydroxyl peaks at 3411 cm−1 and 3418 cm−1 were ascribed to the hydroxyl groups of absorbed water. Fig. 2(e) and (f) were the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution diagrams of Eu-MOF and BA-CDs@Eu-MOF respectively. After BA-CDs introduction, the specific surface area of the material decreased from 39.83 m²·g⁻¹ to 28.63 m²·g⁻¹, and the average pore size decreased from 2.52 nm to 2.34 nm. These results convincingly proved the CDs have been encapsulated into the pores of Eu-MOF.

The fluorescence emission spectra of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF were measured under various excitation wavelengths, and the results were illustrated in Fig. S1. The excitation wavelength had a relatively less effect on the fluorescence intensity at 423 nm, but a more pronounced effect on the emission intensity at 615 nm. After comprehensively considering all factors, 305 nm was determined to be the optimal excitation wavelength. Fig. S2 presented the emission spectra of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF of different concentrations excited at 305 nm. It was observed that the fluorescence intensities at 423 nm and 615 nm increased with increasing MOF concentration. Notably, at a concentration of 0.1 mg·mL⁻¹ , the ratio of fluorescence intensities at 423 nm and 615 nm reached the maximum. Therefore, 0.1 mg·mL⁻¹ was selected as the optimal concentration of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF. Additionally, we also recorded the excitation and emission spectra of the BA-CDs@Eu-MOF probe ( Fig. 3(a)). The emission peak of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF at 423 nm corresponded to the fluorescence emission of BA-CDs, while the characteristic emissions at 590, 615 and 690 nm were attributed to the transitions of 5D0 → 7F1, 5D0 → 7F2 and 5D0 → 7F4 of Eu3+ [58,60], respectively. The fluorescence properties of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF in the presence and absence of CA were investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 3(b), the addition of CA resulted in a significant enhancement of the fluorescence peak of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF at 423 nm and an obvious quenching of the fluorescence peak at 615 nm. To evaluate the stability of the BA-CDs@Eu-MOF suspension under varying conditions, we measured the emission spectra and the fluorescence intensity ratio (I423/I615) of the suspension under different experimental conditions. As illustrated in Fig. S3, fluorescence emission spectra and the I423/I615 ratio of the dispersion of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF only showed negligible changes in 15 h. This observation underscored the exceptional stability of this sensor in aqueous environments. Fig. S4 showed effect of ionic strength on the emission spectra and the I423/I615 ratio of the BA-CDs@Eu-MOF suspension. It was found that when NaCl concentration increased from 0 to 0.6 M the emission spectra and the I423/I615 ratio of the BA-CDs@Eu-MOF suspension hardly changed. These findings further confirmed the remarkable stability of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF under conditions of high ionic strength. Fig. S5 still displayed the emission spectra and the ratio I423/I615 of the BA-CDs@Eu-MOF suspension basically remained stable in the pH range of 5–10, indicating its applicability in both acidic and basic environments. Finally, response of the probe material toward CA was studied. As shown in Fig. S6, the addition of CA resulted in a rapid increase of the I423/I615 value of the system, and the ratio reached the miximum in 0.5 min and then maintained stable. This behavior demonstrated that BA-CDs@Eu-MOF had a rapid response to CA. In summary, BA-CDs@Eu-MOF not only possessed excellent luminescence stability in complex environment but also swift response to the target molecule, further affirming its significant potential for practical applications.

Boric acid, a weak Lewis acid, exhibits a high affinity for CA. In alkaline aqueous solution, boronic acid can interact with ortho dihydroxy group. As a result, pH maybe has an influence on the binding ability between BA-CD and CA. Therefore, we measured the fluorescence intensity ratio (I423/I615) of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF solution in the presence of CA with pH value. As depicted in Fig. S7, with the pH increasing from 5 to 7.5, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I423/I615) gradually increased. When pH was between 7.5 and 9, the ratio was biggest and stable. But when pH exceeded 9, the ratio started to decline slightly. These results indicated that weak alkaline condition was conducive to enhancing the detection sensitivity through the reaction between the adjacent bihydroxyl groups of caffeic acid (CA) and boric groups in BA-CDs. Therefore, pH = 8 was selected as the most appropriate pH value for CA detection in the subsequent experiments.

The responses of BA-CDs and Eu-MOF toward CA also were studied and compared in Fig. S8. With the increasing CA concentration, the fluorescence of BA-CDs at 423 nm gradually enhanced, while the fluorescence of Eu-MOF at 615 nm gradually weakened. It can be seen their relative fluorescence intensity (I-I0)/I0 also linearly changed with CA concentration, where I0 refers to the fluorescence intensity of the blank solution and I refers to the fluorescence intensity in the presence of CA. But correlation coefficient R2 of BA-CDs system was too small, implying relatively poorer accuracy. On the other hand, Eu MOF alone showed lower sensitivity. Comparatively specking, the composite material BA-CDs@Eu-MOF showed better detection performance toward CA. It not only presented low detection limit but also had a self-calibration function. It outputs signals by detecting the ratio of the fluorescence intensities at two different wavelengths (I423/I615), which can largely offset external interferences and reflect the concentration changes of CA more accurately. BA-CDs@Eu-MOF can also improve the detection sensitivity. In addition, CA caused sharp color change from red to blue also is more suitable to smartphone-assisted colorimetric analysis for self-calibration.

Under optimized experimental conditions (Addition amount of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF is 0.1 mg mL−1, pH = 8, excitation wavelength is 305 nm with excitation and emission slits of 5 nm, the detection is performed 10 min after CA addition), fluorescence sensing of the probe to CA was studied. Fig. 4(a, b) illustrated 2D and 3D fluorescence emission spectra of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF in the presence of different concentrations of CA. As the CA concentration increased, the fluorescence emission peak of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF at 423 nm gradually enhanced, while the fluorescence at 615 nm was quenched accordingly. As shown in Fig. 4(c), there was a significant linear relationship between the CA concentration from 0 to 60 μM and the fluorescence intensity ratio I423/I615 (R² = 0.9972). The detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 0.079 μM through the equation LOD = 3σ/k, where σ represents the standard deviation of 10 blank samples and k is the slope of the standard curve). Table S1 compared analytical performances of the BA-CDs@Eu-MOF probe to CA with recently reported MOF-based sensors including fluorescent and electrochemical. It was found that our proposed BA-CDs@Eu-MOF sensor was obviously mor sensitive than the most reported results, for example, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) and poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) composite-based electrochemical sensor (LOD = 1.9 μM) [61] and glutathione (GSH) and thioglycolic acid (TGA) co-capped CdTe quantum dots fluorescent sensor (LOD = 1.2 μM) [62].

The selectivity of the BA-CDs@Eu-MOF probe for CA also was investigated and the fluorescence signals of the probe in the presence of various potentially interfering substances were measured. The results indicated that only CA significantly affect the fluorescence peak of the BA-CDs@Eu-MOF probe, demonstrating its excellent selectivity (Fig. 4d). Most notably, some structural analogues of CA, such as pyrogallic acid and catechol, almost had no interference toward CA detection. To clarify the reasons behind it, we measured and compared absorption spectrum of pyrogallic acid and excitation and emission spectra of the probe material. As show in Fig. S9, the absorption peaks of pyrogallic acid do not overlap with the absorption, excitation and emission spectra of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF, which meant pyrogallic acid did not influence photophysical process of the probe material. For catechol, it only slightly enhanced the fluorescence at 423 nm but had no impact on that at 615 nm. Moreover, when the concentration of catechol was 10 times that of CA, the fluorescence intensity of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF at 423 nm was still lower than that in the presence of CA. Additionally, the anti-interference capability of the BA-CDs@Eu-MOF probe for the detection of CA was evaluated. Fig. 4(e) showed the fluorescence response of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF to CA in the presence of various interfering substances. The results revealed that the presence of these coexisting substances only had a relatively minor effect on the detection of CA, suggesting that the BA-CDs@Eu-MOF probe possessed a robust anti-interference performance for the fluorescence analysis of CA.

Finally, the CIE chromaticity coordinate diagram was drawn in Fig. S10 which demonstrated that the fluorescence color transitioned gradually from red to blue upon the incremental addition of CA, which was also consistent with the expected theoretical color change.

To investigate the fluorescence mechanism, we first compared the PXRD patterns of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF before and after CA treatment. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the characteristic diffraction peaks remained almost unchanged after being treated with CA. Moreover, from Fig. S11, it was observed that after CA treatment SEM morphology of the probe material almost maintained its original characteristics. The results demonstrated that the addition of CA had no effect on the morphology and structure of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF. Therefore, the fluorescence changes of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF after the addition of CA were not caused by structural collapse. Next, to investigate the mechanism of the characteristic emission quenching of Eu3+ at 615 nm, we compared the absorption spectrum of CA and the excitation and emission spectra of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF in Fig. 5(b). It was found that the absorption spectrum of CA overlapped with the excitation spectrum but did not overlap with the emission spectrum. Therefore, the quenching of the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+ in BA-CDs@Eu-MOF might be caused by the inner filter effect (IFE), and the possibility of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) was excluded [63]. To further prove this conclusion, the fluorescence lifetimes of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF at 615 nm before and after the addition of CA were detected (Fig. 5(c)). It was seen that the fluorescence lifetime remained almost unchanged before and after the addition of CA. This result further confirmed that the fluorescence quenching at 615 nm might be due to IFE rather than FRET. To judge the possibility of photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [64], the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels of H3BTC and CA were calculated using density functional theory (DFT)/B3LYP/6–31 G* . As shown in Fig. 5(d), the LUMO energy level of H3BTC (-1.98 eV) was lower than that of CA (-1.66 eV), indicating that electrons could not transfer from the LUMO of H3BTC to that of CA. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF at 615 nm was not caused by PET. Based on the experiments described above it was reasonable to infer that the quenching of Eu³⁺ fluorescence in BA-CDs@Eu-MOF induced by CA could be ascribed to the inner filter effect (IFE).

Next, the mechanism of the fluorescence enhancement of BA-CDs at 423 nm was studied. The zeta potentials of Eu-MOF and BA-CDs@Eu-MOF were measured to be 4.79 and 16.6 mV respectively (Fig. S12). Generally, negatively charged groups can reduce the probability of non-radiative transitions by changing the orbital arrangement of molecules, weakening electron-electron interactions and increasing the probability of fluorescence, thus resulting in enhanced fluorescence [65]. To clarify the interaction between CA and BA-CDs@Eu-MOF, CA caused changes in IR and UV-Vis absorption spectra of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF probe were investigated in detail. As shown in Fig. 5(e), after CA treatment, the IR characteristic peak of B-O-H at 1048 cm⁻¹ vanished, suggesting that an esterification reaction occurred between the boronic acid group and the adjacent dihydroxy group in CA. At the same time, the absorption peak of BA-CDs at 266 nm also disappeared and the absorption peak of CA at 316 nm obviously red-shifted (Fig. 5(f)). These phenomena convincingly confirmed that the o-bihydroxyl group of CA has successfully combined with the boric acid of BA-CDs. The free CA molecule was captured by BA-CDs by the double boronic ester bonds [65]. Then, the captured CA was fixed on the surface of BA-CDs@Eu-MOF by hydrogen bond interaction between the remaining -OH groups on CA and the remaining -OH groups on BA-CDs or the uncoordinated -OH groups in the MOF ligands. This binding interaction effectively suppressed the bond vibrations in BA-CDs, particularly the intramolecular rotation along the single bonds connecting the planar moieties. As a result, there was a notable enhancement in the fluorescence emission of BA-CDs at 423 nm.

As demonstrated in Table 1, the detection of CA in real samples (coffee, green tea, tap water and Yellow River water) was conducted using standard addition method to assess the feasibility and adaptability of the BA-CDs@Eu-MOF fluorescent probe for real sample analysis. The recoveries ranged from 97.6 % to 104.8 %, and relative standard deviations (RSDs) were less than 3.6 %. The detected caffeic acid levels in coffee bean and green tea were 770 ± 0.20 and 449 ± 0.18 mg/kg. These results indicated that the material was suitable for monitoring CA in real samples with satisfactory results.

Fig. 6 showed the smartphone-assisted fluorescence detection process based on BA-CDs@Eu-MOF for the real-time detection of CA. Under the excitation of 365 nm ultraviolet light, the color of the BA-CDs@Eu-MOF probe gradually changed from red to blue after the addition of CA at different concentrations. Photographs of all solutions containing different CA concentrations under ultraviolet lamp were taken and then the RGB values were extracted using color recognition software. Plotting R/B value versus CA concentration, it was found that there was a good linear relationship between R/B and the concentration of CA within the concentration range of 0–60 μM. The linear fitting equation was R/B = -0.0148cCA + 1.780 (R² = 0.9980). According to 3σ/k, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.533 μM. The results demonstrated that the BA-CDs@Eu-MOF probe could be used for the visual detection of CA. Particularly, the sensing platform was rapid, simple and intelligent, which was very conductive to real-time analysis of CA.

In summary, a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe BA-CDs@Eu-MOF was prepared by encapsulating BA-CDs within Eu-MOF. This composite material exhibited remarkable luminescent properties and stability. When utilized for the detection of CA, the BA-CDs@Eu-MOF probe simultaneously demonstrated an enhancement in blue fluorescence at 423 nm and a quenching of the red fluorescence at 615 nm and the resultant fluorescence intensity ratio was exploited as output signal for quantitative detection of CA with a wide linear range (0–60 μM) and low detection limit of 0.079 μM. Furthermore, this probe was successfully used to detect CA in real samples, including coffee, green tea, tap water and Yellow river water, yielding satisfactory recovery rates. At the same time, benefitted from sharp color change from red to blue caused by CA, the probe material also was employed as a smartphone-assisted colorimetric sensing platform for rapid and real-time analysis of CA. This work offers a novel and general strategy for CA detection based on luminescent MOF materials.
